[3] (docs/logfire.md § Pydantic Logfire)
## Pydantic Logfire
## Pydantic Logfire

[Pydantic Logfire](https://pydantic.dev/logfire) is an observability platform developed by the team who created and maintain Pydantic Validation and Pydantic AI. Logfire aims to let you understand your entire application: Gen AI, classic predictive AI, HTTP traffic, database queries and everything else a modern application needs, all using OpenTelemetry.

!!! tip "Pydantic Logfire is a commercial product"
    Logfire is a commercially supported, hosted platform with an extremely generous and perpetual [free tier](https://pydantic.dev/pricing/).
    You can sign up and start using Logfire in a couple of minutes. Logfire can also be self-hosted on the enterprise tier.

Pydantic AI has built-in (but optional) support for Logfire. That means if the `logfire` package is installed and configured and agent instrumentation is enabled then detailed information about agent runs is sent to Logfire. Otherwise there's virtually no overhead and nothing is sent.

Here's an example showing details of running the [Weather Agent](examples/weather-agent.md) in Logfire:

[4] (docs/evals/how-to/logfire-integration.md § Logfire Integration)
## Logfire Integration
# Logfire Integration

Visualize and analyze evaluation results using Pydantic Logfire.

## Overview
## Overview

Pydantic Evals uses OpenTelemetry to record traces of the evaluation process. These traces contain all the information from your evaluation reports, plus full tracing from the execution of your task function.

You can send these traces to any OpenTelemetry-compatible backend, including [Pydantic Logfire](https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/guides/web-ui/evals/).

## Installation
## Installation

Install the optional logfire dependency:

```bash
pip install 'pydantic-evals[logfire]'
```

[5] (docs/evals.md § Code-First Evaluation)
## Code-First Evaluation
## Code-First Evaluation

Pydantic Evals follows a **code-first approach** where you define all evaluation components (datasets, experiments, tasks, cases and evaluators) in Python code, or as serialized data loaded by Python code. This differs from platforms with fully web-based configuration.

When you run an _Experiment_ you'll see a progress indicator and can print the results wherever you run your python code (IDE, terminal, etc). You also get a report object back that you can serialize and store or send to a notebook or other application for further visualization and analysis.

If you are using [Pydantic Logfire](https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/guides/web-ui/evals/), your experiment results automatically appear in the Logfire web interface for visualization, comparison, and collaborative analysis. Logfire serves as a observability layer - you write and run evals in code, then view and analyze results in the web UI.

# Logfire UI Display for Pydantic AI

To get Pydantic AI agent analyses to appear in the Logfire UI, follow these steps:

## Setup

Configure Logfire instrumentation in your code [1]:

```python
import logfire

logfire.configure()
logfire.instrument_pydantic_ai()
```

## What Gets Displayed

Once instrumented, every agent run creates a detailed trace in Logfire showing [1]:

- **Messages exchanged** with the model (system, user, assistant)
- **Tool calls** including arguments and return values
- **Token usage** per request and cumulative
- **Latency** for each operation
- **Errors** with full context

## How It Appears

The documentation includes a video demonstration showing how Logfire visualizes agent flow [2]. There's also a real-world example of the Weather Agent running in Logfire [3].

Additionally, you can use Logfire's SQL querying capability to monitor performance metrics of your application [2].

## Note

Pydantic Logfire is a commercially supported platform with a generous free tier. It's built on OpenTelemetry, so you can also send traces to any OpenTelemetry-compatible backend [3].

For more details on configuring Logfire itself, see the [Logfire documentation](https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/) [2].

[1] (docs/evals/how-to/concurrency.md § Rate Limiting)
## Rate Limiting
### Rate Limiting

Many APIs have rate limits that restrict concurrent requests:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

async def my_llm_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'LLM Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='rate_limit_demo', cases=[Case(inputs='test1')])

# If your API allows 10 requests/second
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(
    my_llm_task,
    max_concurrency=10,
)
```

## Resource Constraints
### Resource Constraints

Limit concurrency to avoid overwhelming system resources:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def heavy_computation(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Heavy: {inputs}'

def db_query_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'DB: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='resource_constraints', cases=[Case(inputs='test1')])

# Memory-intensive operations
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(
    heavy_computation,
    max_concurrency=2,  # Only 2 at a time
)

# Database connection pool limits
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(
    db_query_task,
    max_concurrency=5,  # Match connection pool size
)
```

[2] (docs/evals/online-evaluation.md § Concurrency Control)
## Concurrency Control
## Concurrency Control

Each [`OnlineEvaluator`][pydantic_evals.online.OnlineEvaluator] has a `max_concurrency` limit (default: 10). When the limit is reached, new evaluation requests for that evaluator are **dropped** (not queued). This prevents expensive evaluators from consuming unbounded resources:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

from pydantic_evals.evaluators import Evaluator, EvaluatorContext
from pydantic_evals.online import OnlineEvaluator

@dataclass
class ExpensiveCheck(Evaluator):
    async def evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> bool:
        # Imagine this makes a slow call to an LLM
        return True

# Allow at most 3 concurrent evaluations
limited = OnlineEvaluator(
    evaluator=ExpensiveCheck(),
    sample_rate=0.1,
    max_concurrency=3,
)
```

[3] (docs/evals/how-to/concurrency.md § Concurrency & Performance)
## Concurrency & Performance
# Concurrency & Performance

Control how evaluation cases are executed in parallel.

## Overview
## Overview

By default, Pydantic Evals runs all cases concurrently to maximize throughput. You can control this behavior using the `max_concurrency` parameter.

## Basic Usage
## Basic Usage

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def my_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='concurrency_demo', cases=[Case(inputs='test1'), Case(inputs='test2')])

# Run all cases concurrently (default)
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task)

# Limit to 5 concurrent cases
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task, max_concurrency=5)

# Run sequentially (one at a time)
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task, max_concurrency=1)
```

## When to Limit Concurrency
## When to Limit Concurrency

[4] (docs/evals/how-to/concurrency.md § Sync API)
## Sync API
### Sync API

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def my_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='sync_demo', cases=[Case(inputs='test1')])

# Runs async operations internally with controlled concurrency
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task, max_concurrency=10)
```

## Async API
### Async API

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

async def my_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

async def run_evaluation():
    dataset = Dataset(name='async_demo', cases=[Case(inputs='test1')])
    # Same behavior, but in async context
    report = await dataset.evaluate(my_task, max_concurrency=10)
    return report
```

[5] (docs/evals/how-to/concurrency.md § Handling Rate Limits)
## Handling Rate Limits
## Handling Rate Limits

If you hit rate limits, the evaluation will fail. Use retry strategies:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='rate_limit_handling', cases=[Case(inputs='test1')])

# Reduce concurrency to avoid rate limits
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(
    task,
    max_concurrency=5,  # Stay under rate limit
```

```python
    'stop_recursing_when': {'name_equals': 'boundary'},
```

QUESTION:
How can I use pydantic-evals HasMatchingSpan evaluators to verify a RAG workflow, including checking that generation happened with retrieved context?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Create a list of HasMatchingSpan evaluators: one with query={'name_contains': 'vector_search'} (evaluation_name='retrieved_docs') for retrieval, one with query={'name_contains': 'rerank'} (evaluation_name='reranked_results') for reranking, and one combining conditions with query={'and_': [{'name_contains': 'generate'}, {'has_attribute_keys': ['context_ids']}]} (evaluation_name='used_context') to confirm generation used context. Import HasMatchingSpan from pydantic_evals.evaluators.

ANSWER A:
# Using HasMatchingSpan for RAG Evaluation

`HasMatchingSpan` evaluators verify that specific text from retrieved context appears in the generated output. Here's how to use them for RAG workflows:

## Basic Setup

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic_evals import HasMatchingSpan

class RAGOutput(BaseModel):
    query: str
    retrieved_context: list[str]
    generation: str

# Create evaluator
span_evaluator = HasMatchingSpan()
```

## Evaluating RAG Workflow

```python
from pydantic_evals import evaluate

rag_output = RAGOutput(
    query="What is Claude?",
    retrieved_context=[
        "Claude is an AI assistant made by Anthropic.",
        "It was trained using constitutional AI methods."
    ],
    generation="Claude is an AI assistant made by Anthropic that uses advanced reasoning capabilities."
)

# Check if retrieved context appears in generation
results = evaluate(
    [rag_output],
    evaluator=HasMatchingSpan(
        haystack_field="generation",
        needle_fields=["retrieved_context"],
    )
)
```

## Comprehensive RAG Verification

```python
from pydantic_evals import Evaluator, evaluate_model

class RAGEvaluator(Evaluator):
    def evaluate(self, output: RAGOutput) -> dict:
        checks = {}
        
        # Verify context retrieval happened
        checks["has_context"] = len(output.retrieved_context) > 0
        
        # Verify key spans from context appear in generation
        for i, context in enumerate(output.retrieved_context):
            span_check = HasMatchingSpan(
                haystack=output.generation,
                needle=context
            )
            checks[f"context_{i}_used"] = span_check.evaluate()
        
        return checks
```

## Key Patterns for RAG

| Check | Implementation |
|-------|-----------------|
| **Context used in output** | `HasMatchingSpan(haystack_field="generation", needle_fields=["retrieved_context"])` |
| **Specific fact verification** | Extract key facts from context, verify they appear in generation |
| **Multi-document consistency** | Check each retrieved document for relevant spans in output |

This ensures your RAG generates outputs grounded in retrieved context rather than hallucinating.

ANSWER B:
# Verifying RAG Workflows with HasMatchingSpan

Use the `HasMatchingSpan` evaluators designed for RAG system verification [1]:

```python
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import HasMatchingSpan

evaluators = [
    # Retrieved documents
    HasMatchingSpan(
        query={'name_contains': 'vector_search'},
        evaluation_name='retrieved_docs',
    ),

    # Reranked results
    HasMatchingSpan(
        query={'name_contains': 'rerank'},
        evaluation_name='reranked_results',
    ),

    # Generated with context
    HasMatchingSpan(
        query={'and_': [
            {'name_contains': 'generate'},
            {'has_attribute_keys': ['context_ids']},
        ]},
        evaluation_name='used_context',
    ),
]
```

## Key Points

The critical evaluator for your use case is the third one: it verifies that **generation happened with retrieved context** by checking that:
1. A span containing `'generate'` exists
2. That span has `context_ids` attribute keys (proving context was used)

The `'and_'` query operator requires both conditions to be true [1].

**Best practice**: Name your spans descriptively in your application code (e.g., `'vector_search'`, `'generate'`) so these queries match reliably [1].

QUESTION:
In pydantic-ai, how are analyses displayed in the Logfire UI, and how do I get them to show up there?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
When Logfire is configured, analyses are automatically attached to the experiment span as the `logfire.experiment.analyses` attribute, and the Logfire UI renders them as interactive visualizations: confusion matrices as heatmaps, precision-recall curves as line charts with AUC in the legend, line plots (ROC curves, KS plots, etc.) as line charts with configurable axes, scalar results as labeled values, and tables as formatted data tables. When comparing multiple experiments in the Logfire Evals view, analyses of the same type are displayed side by side.

ANSWER A:
# Displaying Analyses in Logfire UI with Pydantic-AI

To get pydantic-ai analyses to show up in Logfire UI:

## 1. Install and Authenticate Logfire

```bash
pip install logfire
logfire auth
```

## 2. Initialize Logfire in Your Code

```python
import logfire
from pydantic_ai import Agent

logfire.configure()

agent = Agent(model='...')
```

## 3. Run Your Agent

```python
result = agent.run_sync('your prompt')
```

Analyses will **automatically appear** in the Logfire dashboard under the trace for that agent run.

## What You'll See

In the Logfire UI:
- **Agent runs** appear as spans in the trace view
- **Analyses** show up as nested spans with structured data
- **Tool calls** and **model interactions** are visible in the hierarchy
- You can expand spans to see details like prompts, responses, and metadata

## Optional: Custom Configuration

If you want more control:

```python
import logfire
from pydantic_ai import Agent

logfire.configure(
    service_name='my-agent',
    service_version='1.0.0'
)

agent = Agent(model='...')
result = agent.run_sync('prompt')
```

## Key Point

You don't need to manually log analyses—pydantic-ai **automatically instruments** calls when Logfire is initialized. Just call `logfire.configure()` and run your agent normally.

See the [Logfire docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev/logfire/) for dashboard navigation and filtering options.

ANSWER B:
# Logfire UI Display for Pydantic AI

To get Pydantic AI agent analyses to appear in the Logfire UI, follow these steps:

## Setup

Configure Logfire instrumentation in your code [1]:

```python
import logfire

logfire.configure()
logfire.instrument_pydantic_ai()
```

## What Gets Displayed

Once instrumented, every agent run creates a detailed trace in Logfire showing [1]:

- **Messages exchanged** with the model (system, user, assistant)
- **Tool calls** including arguments and return values
- **Token usage** per request and cumulative
- **Latency** for each operation
- **Errors** with full context

## How It Appears

The documentation includes a video demonstration showing how Logfire visualizes agent flow [2]. There's also a real-world example of the Weather Agent running in Logfire [3].

Additionally, you can use Logfire's SQL querying capability to monitor performance metrics of your application [2].

## Note

Pydantic Logfire is a commercially supported platform with a generous free tier. It's built on OpenTelemetry, so you can also send traces to any OpenTelemetry-compatible backend [3].

For more details on configuring Logfire itself, see the [Logfire documentation](https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/) [2].

# Adding Cases to a Pydantic-Evals Dataset

In pydantic-evals, you can create a Dataset with initial Cases and then add more cases later:

## Initial Dataset Creation

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Case

# Create initial cases
initial_cases = [
    Case(input="test input 1", expected_output="expected output 1"),
    Case(input="test input 2", expected_output="expected output 2"),
]

# Create dataset with initial cases
dataset = Dataset(cases=initial_cases)
```

## Adding Cases Later

```python
# Add a new case after discovering an issue
new_case = Case(input="test input 3", expected_output="expected output 3")
dataset.cases.append(new_case)
```

Or if there's a dedicated method (check your version):

```python
dataset.add_case(Case(input="test input 3", expected_output="expected output 3"))
```

## Full Example

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Case

# Start with initial cases
dataset = Dataset(cases=[
    Case(input="hello", expected_output="world"),
    Case(input="foo", expected_output="bar"),
])

# Later, add more cases as you find issues
dataset.cases.append(Case(input="edge case", expected_output="expected"))
```

**Note:** If `dataset.cases` isn't directly mutable in your version, check the library's documentation for the specific method to add cases (it may vary by version).

## Loading from YAML
### Loading from YAML

```python
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected, IsInstance

# First create and save the dataset
Path('my_tests.yaml').parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any](
    name='my_tests',
    cases=[Case(name='test_1', inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO')],
    evaluators=[IsInstance(type_name='str'), EqualsExpected()],
)
dataset.to_file('my_tests.yaml')

# Load the dataset with type parameters
dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_file('my_tests.yaml')

def my_task(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Run evaluation
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task)
```

## JSON Format
## JSON Format

JSON format is useful for programmatic generation or when strict structure is required.

```python
- name: test
  inputs: hello
  expected_output: HELLO
evaluators:
- EqualsExpected
"""

dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_text(yaml_content, fmt='yaml')
```

```python
}

dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_dict(data)
```

[4] (docs/evals/how-to/dataset-serialization.md § Dataset Serialization)
## Dataset Serialization
# Dataset Serialization

Learn how to save and load datasets in different formats, with support for custom evaluators and IDE integration.

## Overview
## Overview

Pydantic Evals supports serializing datasets to files in two formats:

- **YAML** (`.yaml`, `.yml`) - Human-readable, great for version control
- **JSON** (`.json`) - Structured, machine-readable

Both formats support:
- Automatic JSON schema generation for IDE autocomplete and validation
- Custom evaluator serialization/deserialization
- Type-safe loading with generic parameters

## YAML Format
## YAML Format

YAML is the recommended format for most use cases due to its readability and compact syntax.

[5] (docs/evals/how-to/dataset-serialization.md § Basic Example)
## Basic Example
### Basic Example

```python
from typing import Any

from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected, IsInstance

# Create a dataset with typed parameters
dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any](
    name='my_tests',
    cases=[
        Case(
            name='test_1',
            inputs='hello',
            expected_output='HELLO',
        ),
    ],
    evaluators=[
        IsInstance(type_name='str'),
        EqualsExpected(),
    ],
)

# Save to YAML
dataset.to_file('my_tests.yaml')
```

This creates two files:

1. **`my_tests.yaml`** - The dataset
2. **`my_tests_schema.json`** - JSON schema for IDE support

## YAML Output
### YAML Output

```yaml
# yaml-language-server: $schema=my_tests_schema.json
name: my_tests
cases:
- name: test_1
  inputs: hello
  expected_output: HELLO
evaluators:
- IsInstance: str
- EqualsExpected
```

raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":8003,"duration_api_ms":9225,"ttft_ms":6674,"ttft_stream_ms":879,"time_to_request_ms":6,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Custom Evaluator Serialization in pydantic-evals

For a custom Evaluator to serialize/deserialize correctly with `to_file()` and `from_file()`, follow these requirements:

## 1. **Inherit from BaseEvaluator**
```python
from pydantic_evals import BaseEvaluator

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    pass
```

## 2. **Use Pydantic-Compatible Types**
All fields must be serializable Pydantic types with proper type hints:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    name: str
    threshold: float
    config: dict
```

## 3. **Avoid Non-Serializable Objects**
- ❌ Don't store: lambdas, file handles, database connections, model instances
- ✅ Store: primitives, strings, dicts, lists, nested Pydantic models

## 4. **Implement Required Methods**
Typically requires implementing the `evaluate()` method:
```python
async def evaluate(self, **kwargs) -> dict:
    # Your evaluation logic
    return {"score": score, "reasoning": reasoning}
```

## 5. **Use Model Config if Needed**
```python
from pydantic import ConfigDict

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=False)
    name: str
```

## Key Principle
**Keep your Evaluator's state purely data-driven** — only store configuration/parameters, not behavior or external dependencies. External dependencies (LLM clients, etc.) should be passed at evaluation time.

# Custom Evaluator Serialization in pydantic-evals

For a custom Evaluator to serialize/deserialize correctly with `to_file()` and `from_file()`, follow these requirements:

## 1. **Inherit from BaseEvaluator**
```python
from pydantic_evals import BaseEvaluator

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    pass
```

## 2. **Use Pydantic-Compatible Types**
All fields must be serializable Pydantic types with proper type hints:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    name: str
    threshold: float
    config: dict
```

## 3. **Avoid Non-Serializable Objects**
- ❌ Don't store: lambdas, file handles, database connections, model instances
- ✅ Store: primitives, strings, dicts, lists, nested Pydantic models

## 4. **Implement Required Methods**
Typically requires implementing the `evaluate()` method:
```python
async def evaluate(self, **kwargs) -> dict:
    # Your evaluation logic
    return {"score": score, "reasoning": reasoning}
```

## 5. **Use Model Config if Needed**
```python
from pydantic import ConfigDict

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=False)
    name: str
```

## Key Principle
**Keep your Evaluator's state purely data-driven** — only store configuration/parameters, not behavior or external dependencies. External dependencies (LLM clients, etc.) should be passed at evaluation time.

prompt: In pydantic-evals, how can I run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare their results in Logfire?
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":8109,"duration_api_ms":9008,"ttft_ms":6531,"ttft_stream_ms":1005,"time_to_request_ms":5,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Running Multiple Task Implementations Against Same Dataset in pydantic-evals

Here's how to run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare results in Logfire:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Task
import logfire

# Initialize Logfire
logfire.configure()

# Create shared dataset once
dataset = Dataset.from_list([
    {"question": "What is 2+2?"},
    {"question": "What is the capital of France?"},
    # ... more examples
])

# Task Implementation 1
class SimpleTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your first implementation
        return {"answer": simple_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Task Implementation 2
class AdvancedTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your second implementation
        return {"answer": advanced_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Run both tasks on same dataset with distinct names
results1 = SimpleTask().eval(dataset, name="simple_approach")
results2 = AdvancedTask().eval(dataset, name="advanced_approach")
```

**Key points:**

# Running Multiple Task Implementations Against Same Dataset in pydantic-evals

Here's how to run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare results in Logfire:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Task
import logfire

# Initialize Logfire
logfire.configure()

# Create shared dataset once
dataset = Dataset.from_list([
    {"question": "What is 2+2?"},
    {"question": "What is the capital of France?"},
    # ... more examples
])

# Task Implementation 1
class SimpleTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your first implementation
        return {"answer": simple_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Task Implementation 2
class AdvancedTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your second implementation
        return {"answer": advanced_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Run both tasks on same dataset with distinct names
results1 = SimpleTask().eval(dataset, name="simple_approach")
results2 = AdvancedTask().eval(dataset, name="advanced_approach")
```

**Key points:**

1. **Create dataset once** - pass the same `Dataset` instance to both tasks
2. **Use distinct `name` parameter** - this identifies each run in Logfire for easy comparison
3. **Logfire automatically captures results** - both runs are logged with metrics, traces, and outputs
4. **Compare in Logfire UI** - view side-by-side results, scores, and performance metrics

In Logfire, you can then:
- View both runs under the same span tree
- Compare success rates, latencies, and outputs
- Filter/search by the `name` parameter to isolate each implementation

## Datasets and Cases
## Datasets and Cases

In Pydantic Evals, everything begins with [`Dataset`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Dataset]s and [`Case`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case]s:

- **[`Dataset`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Dataset]**: A collection of test Cases designed for the evaluation of a specific task or function
- **[`Case`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case]**: A single test scenario corresponding to Task inputs, with optional expected outputs, metadata, and case-specific evaluators

```python {title="simple_eval_dataset.py"}
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

case1 = Case(
    name='simple_case',
    inputs='What is the capital of France?',
    expected_output='Paris',
    metadata={'difficulty': 'easy'},
)

dataset = Dataset(name='capital_quiz', cases=[case1])
```

_(This example is complete, it can be run "as is")_

See [Dataset Management](evals/how-to/dataset-management.md) to learn about saving, loading, and generating datasets.

## Adding More Cases

```python {test="skip"}
    expected_output='Café Résumé',
)

# Found a bug with all caps words

    name='acronyms',

# Test with very long input

)

# Save updated dataset
dataset.to_file('title_case_tests.yaml')
```

## Case
## Case

A [`Case`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case] represents a single test scenario with specific inputs and optional expected outputs.

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

case = Case(
    name='test_uppercase',  # Optional, but recommended for reporting
    inputs='hello world',  # Required: inputs to your task
    expected_output='HELLO WORLD',  # Optional: expected output
    metadata={'category': 'basic'},  # Optional: arbitrary metadata
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],  # Optional: case-specific evaluators
)
```

## 3. Start Small, Grow Gradually
### 3. Start Small, Grow Gradually

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

# Start with representative cases
dataset = Dataset(
    name='starting_small',
    cases=[
        Case(name='happy_path', inputs='test'),
        Case(name='edge_case', inputs=''),
        Case(name='error_case', inputs='invalid'),
    ],
)

# Add more as you find issues
dataset.add_case(name='newly_discovered_edge_case', inputs='edge')
```

[5] (docs/evals/quick-start.md § Quick Start)
## Quick Start
```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import Contains, EqualsExpected

# Create a dataset with test cases
dataset = Dataset(
    name='uppercase_tests',
    cases=[
        Case(
            name='uppercase_basic',
            inputs='hello world',
            expected_output='HELLO WORLD',
        ),
        Case(
            name='uppercase_with_numbers',
            inputs='hello 123',
            expected_output='HELLO 123',
        ),
    ],
    evaluators=[
        EqualsExpected(),  # Check exact match with expected_output
        Contains(value='HELLO', case_sensitive=True),  # Check contains "HELLO"
    ],
)

# Define the function to evaluate
def uppercase_text(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Run the evaluation
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(uppercase_text)

# Print the results
report.print()
"""
        Evaluation Summary: uppercase_text
┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Case ID                ┃ Assertions ┃ Duration ┃

│ uppercase_basic        │ ✔✔         │     10ms │
├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────┤

├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────┤
│ Averages               │ 100.0% ✔   │     10ms │
└────────────────────────┴────────────┴──────────┘
"""
```

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
```

QUESTION:
How do I load a pydantic-evals Dataset from a YAML string or from a Python dict?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Use `Dataset[str, str, Any].from_text(yaml_content, fmt='yaml')` to load from a YAML string, or `Dataset[str, str, Any].from_dict(data)` to load from a Python dict. The data should contain a `name`, a `cases` list (each with `name`, `inputs`, `expected_output`), and `evaluators` (e.g. `EqualsExpected`).

ANSWER A:
# Loading from YAML String and Dict

## From YAML String

Use the `from_text()` method with `fmt='yaml'` [2]:

```python
from typing import Any
from pydantic_evals import Dataset

yaml_content = """
name: my_tests
cases:
- name: test
  inputs: hello
  expected_output: HELLO
evaluators:
- EqualsExpected
"""

dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_text(yaml_content, fmt='yaml')
```

## From Python Dict

Use the `from_dict()` method [2]:

```python
from typing import Any
from pydantic_evals import Dataset

data = {
    'name': 'my_tests',
    'cases': [
        {
            'name': 'test',
            'inputs': 'hello',
            'expected_output': 'HELLO',
        },
    ],
    'evaluators': [{'EqualsExpected': {}}],
}

dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_dict(data)
```

Both methods support type parameters (`[str, str, Any]`) for type-safe loading.

ANSWER B:

- **Pydantic v2**: `.model_validate()`

# Per-Case Setup in pydantic-ai's pydantic_evals

In pydantic-ai's evaluation framework, you can use the **`setup`** parameter with a callable that receives each case. Here's the pattern:

```python
from pydantic_ai.eval import Case, Evaluator

def per_case_setup(case: Case) -> dict:
    """Read metadata and compute values for this case."""
    computed_value = case.metadata.get('some_key') or compute_default()
    return {
        'computed_field': computed_value,
        'case_id': case.id,
    }

# Pass setup to your evaluator
evaluator = Evaluator(
    setup=per_case_setup,
    # ... other config
)
```

The setup function:
- Receives the `Case` (with `.metadata` accessible)
- Returns a dict of computed values
- These values are injected into evaluator context/metrics before evaluation runs

## Accessing Setup Values in Metrics

Within your evaluator metrics/models, access the injected values via the context passed to them:

```python
def my_metric(result, setup_context: dict) -> float:
    """Metric function receives setup_context."""
    computed_value = setup_context.get('computed_field')
    # Use in evaluation logic
    return score
```

## Alternative: Context/State Management

If you need more control, you might also use a context manager approach:

```python
class PerCaseEvaluator:
    def setup(self, case: Case):
        self.context = {'metadata': case.metadata, 'computed': ...}
```

**Note:** If you need the exact API details, check the current [pydantic-ai documentation](https://ai.pydantic.dev/) as evaluation APIs may evolve. The `setup` parameter is the standard pattern, but verify the exact signature and context-passing mechanism for your version.

QUESTION:
In pydantic-evals, how do I create a Dataset with some initial Cases and then add another case later after I find new issues?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Create a Dataset by passing a name and a list of Case objects (e.g. `Dataset(name='starting_small', cases=[Case(name='happy_path', inputs='test'), ...])`), then add more cases later using `dataset.add_case(name='newly_discovered_edge_case', inputs='edge')`.

ANSWER A:
To create a Dataset with initial Cases and add more later, use the `add_case()` method [2]:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

# Create dataset with initial cases
dataset = Dataset(
    name='my_tests',
    cases=[
        Case(name='case1', inputs='input1', expected_output='output1'),
        Case(name='case2', inputs='input2', expected_output='output2'),
    ],
)

# Add new cases as you find issues
dataset.add_case(
    name='new_edge_case',
    inputs='edge_input',
    expected_output='edge_output',
)

# Save the updated dataset
dataset.to_file('my_tests.yaml')
```

You can also load an existing dataset and add cases to it [2]:

```python
# Load existing dataset
dataset = Dataset.from_file('my_tests.yaml')

# Add new case
dataset.add_case(name='unicode_test', inputs='café', expected_output='Café')

# Save updated dataset
dataset.to_file('my_tests.yaml')
```

This pattern of starting small and growing gradually is recommended for managing test datasets over time [4].

ANSWER B:
# Adding Cases to a Pydantic-Evals Dataset

In pydantic-evals, you can create a Dataset with initial Cases and then add more cases later:

## Initial Dataset Creation

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Case

# Create initial cases
initial_cases = [
    Case(input="test input 1", expected_output="expected output 1"),
    Case(input="test input 2", expected_output="expected output 2"),
]

# Create dataset with initial cases
dataset = Dataset(cases=initial_cases)
```

## Adding Cases Later

```python
# Add a new case after discovering an issue
new_case = Case(input="test input 3", expected_output="expected output 3")
dataset.cases.append(new_case)
```

Or if there's a dedicated method (check your version):

```python
dataset.add_case(Case(input="test input 3", expected_output="expected output 3"))
```

## Full Example

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Case

# Start with initial cases
dataset = Dataset(cases=[
    Case(input="hello", expected_output="world"),
    Case(input="foo", expected_output="bar"),
])

# Later, add more cases as you find issues
dataset.cases.append(Case(input="edge case", expected_output="expected"))
```

## Multiple Experiments from One Dataset
### Multiple Experiments from One Dataset

A key feature of Pydantic Evals is that you can run the same dataset against different task implementations:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='comparison_test',
    cases=[
        Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO'),
    ],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

# Original implementation
def task_v1(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Improved implementation (with exclamation)
def task_v2(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper() + '!'

# Compare results
report_v1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v1)
report_v2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v2)

avg_v1 = report_v1.averages()
avg_v2 = report_v2.averages()
print(f'V1 pass rate: {avg_v1.assertions if avg_v1 and avg_v1.assertions else 0}')
#> V1 pass rate: 1.0
print(f'V2 pass rate: {avg_v2.assertions if avg_v2 and avg_v2.assertions else 0}')
#> V2 pass rate: 0
```

This allows you to:

## Comparing Runs
### Comparing Runs

Run the same evaluation multiple times and compare in Logfire:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def original_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'original result for {inputs}'

def improved_task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'improved result for {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='comparison', cases=[Case(name='test', inputs='example')])

# Run 1: Original implementation
report1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(original_task)

# Run 2: Improved implementation
report2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(improved_task)

# Compare in Logfire by filtering by timestamp or attributes
```

A [`Case`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case] represents a single test scenario with specific inputs and optional expected outputs.

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case
```

## Code-First Evaluation
## Code-First Evaluation

Pydantic Evals follows a **code-first approach** where you define all evaluation components (datasets, experiments, tasks, cases and evaluators) in Python code, or as serialized data loaded by Python code. This differs from platforms with fully web-based configuration.

When you run an _Experiment_ you'll see a progress indicator and can print the results wherever you run your python code (IDE, terminal, etc). You also get a report object back that you can serialize and store or send to a notebook or other application for further visualization and analysis.

If you are using [Pydantic Logfire](https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/guides/web-ui/evals/), your experiment results automatically appear in the Logfire web interface for visualization, comparison, and collaborative analysis. Logfire serves as a observability layer - you write and run evals in code, then view and analyze results in the web UI.

# Running Multiple Task Implementations Against One Dataset in Logfire

To run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare their results in Logfire, follow these steps:

## 1. Configure Logfire Early [3]

```python
import logfire

logfire.configure(
    service_name='rag-pipeline-evals',  # Use a descriptive name
    environment='development',
)
```

## 2. Define Your Dataset and Task Implementations [1]

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='comparison_test',
    cases=[
        Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO'),
    ],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

# Original implementation
def task_v1(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Improved implementation
def task_v2(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper() + '!'
```

## 3. Run Both Implementations and Compare [1][2]

```python
# Run 1: Original implementation
report_v1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v1)

# Run 2: Improved implementation
report_v2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v2)

# Compare results programmatically
avg_v1 = report_v1.averages()
avg_v2 = report_v2.averages()
```

## 4. Compare in Logfire [2]

Both runs will automatically be captured in Logfire. You can then **compare them in the Logfire web UI by filtering by timestamp or attributes** to analyze the performance differences between your implementations.

## With Custom Evaluators
### With Custom Evaluators

When loading datasets that use custom evaluators, you must pass them to `from_file()`:

```python {test="skip"}
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

from pydantic_evals import Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import Evaluator, EvaluatorContext

@dataclass
class MyCustomEvaluator(Evaluator):
    threshold: float = 0.5

    def evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> bool:
        return True

# Load with custom evaluator registry
dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_file(
    'my_dataset.yaml',
    custom_evaluator_types=[MyCustomEvaluator],
)
```

For complete details on serialization with custom evaluators, see [Dataset Serialization](dataset-serialization.md).

## Module api/pydantic_ai_slim/pydantic_ai/_spec.py
# Module api/pydantic_ai_slim/pydantic_ai/_spec.py

Shared spec utilities for constructing objects from YAML/JSON/dict specifications.

This module provides the `NamedSpec` class (generalized from `EvaluatorSpec` in pydantic_evals)
and registry/loading utilities that can be reused by both the evaluator system and the capability system.

## serializes_as_string_keyed_dict(value)
## serializes_as_string_keyed_dict(value)

Check if a value would serialize to a dict with all string keys.

When serialize() uses the compact tuple form (arguments = (value,)), the serialized
output becomes {Name: value}. On deserialization, _SerializedNamedSpec._args
treats any dict with all-string keys as kwargs. This means a single positional argument
that is itself a dict (like ModelSettings) would be incorrectly unpacked as kwargs
on the round-trip. We avoid the compact form in this case.

[4] (docs/evals/evaluators/custom.md § Custom Evaluators)
## Custom Evaluators
# Custom Evaluators

Write custom evaluators for domain-specific logic, external integrations, or specialized metrics.

## Basic Custom Evaluator
## Basic Custom Evaluator

All evaluators inherit from [`Evaluator`][pydantic_evals.evaluators.Evaluator] and must implement `evaluate`:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

from pydantic_evals.evaluators import Evaluator, EvaluatorContext

@dataclass
class ExactMatch(Evaluator):
    """Check if output exactly matches expected output."""

    def evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> bool:
        return ctx.output == ctx.expected_output
```

**Key Points:**

- Use `@dataclass` decorator (required)
- Inherit from `Evaluator`
- Implement `evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> EvaluatorOutput`
- Return `bool`, `int`, `float`, `str`, [`EvaluationReason`][pydantic_evals.evaluators.EvaluationReason], or `dict` of these

[5] (docs/changelog.md § v0.x.x)
## v0.x.x
See [#1799](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/pull/1799) - Pydantic Evals `EvaluationReport` and `ReportCase` are now generic dataclasses instead of Pydantic models. If you were serializing them using `model_dump()`, you will now need to use the `EvaluationReportAdapter` and `ReportCaseAdapter` type adapters instead.

See [#1507](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/pull/1507) - The `ToolDefinition` `description` argument is now optional and the order of positional arguments has changed from `name, description, parameters_json_schema, ...` to `name, parameters_json_schema, description, ...` to account for this.

**v0.3.0 (2025-06-18)**

See [#1142](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/pull/1142) — Adds support for thinking parts.

# Custom Evaluator Serialization Requirements

To create a custom Evaluator that serializes and deserializes correctly with `to_file()` and `from_file()`, you must meet these requirements:

## Implementation Requirements [4]

1. **Use `@dataclass` decorator** (required)
2. **Inherit from `Evaluator`**
3. **Implement `evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext)` method**

Example:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import Evaluator, EvaluatorContext

@dataclass
class MyCustomEvaluator(Evaluator):
    threshold: float = 0.5
    
    def evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> bool:
        return True
```

## Registration Requirements

**When saving:** Pass your custom evaluator type to `to_file()` [1]
```python
dataset.to_file(
    'test_cases.yaml',
    custom_report_evaluator_types=[MyCustomReportEvaluator],
)
```

**When loading:** Pass your custom evaluator type to `from_file()` [2]
```python
dataset = Dataset[str, str, Any].from_file(
    'my_dataset.yaml',
    custom_evaluator_types=[MyCustomEvaluator],
)
```

The key requirement is registering your custom evaluator types with both `to_file()` and `from_file()` so the JSON schema can properly serialize and deserialize them.

[1] (docs/evals/how-to/metrics-attributes.md § Metrics vs Attributes vs Metadata)
## Metrics vs Attributes vs Metadata
```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset, increment_eval_metric, set_eval_attribute

# Case Metadata: Defined in case (before execution)
case = Case(
    inputs='question',
    metadata={'difficulty': 'hard', 'category': 'math'},  # Per-case metadata
)

dataset = Dataset(name='metrics_demo', cases=[case])

# Metrics & Attributes: Recorded during execution
async def task(inputs):
    # These are recorded during execution for each case
    increment_eval_metric('tokens', 100)
    set_eval_attribute('model', 'gpt-5.2')
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

async def main():
    # Experiment Metadata: Defined at evaluation time
    await dataset.evaluate(
        task,
        metadata={  # Experiment-level metadata
            'prompt_version': 'v2.1',
            'temperature': 0.7,
        },
    )
```

## Per-Case Setup and Teardown
```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators.context import EvaluatorContext
from pydantic_evals.lifecycle import CaseLifecycle
from pydantic_evals.reporting import ReportCase, ReportCaseFailure

class SetupFromMetadata(CaseLifecycle[str, str, dict]):
    async def setup(self) -> None:
        prefix = (self.case.metadata or {}).get('prefix', '')
        self.prefix = prefix

    async def prepare_context(
        self, ctx: EvaluatorContext[str, str, dict]
    ) -> EvaluatorContext[str, str, dict]:
        ctx.metrics['prefix_length'] = len(self.prefix)
        return ctx

    async def teardown(
        self,
        result: ReportCase[str, str, dict] | ReportCaseFailure[str, str, dict] | None,
    ) -> None:
        pass  # Clean up resources here

dataset = Dataset(
    name='setup_teardown',
    cases=[
        Case(name='no_prefix', inputs='hello', metadata={'prefix': ''}),
        Case(name='with_prefix', inputs='hello', metadata={'prefix': 'PREFIX:'}),
    ]
)

report = dataset.evaluate_sync(lambda inputs: inputs.upper(), lifecycle=SetupFromMetadata)

metrics = {c.name: c.metrics for c in report.cases}
print(metrics['no_prefix']['prefix_length'])
#> 0
print(metrics['with_prefix']['prefix_length'])
#> 7
```

## Per-Case Setup and Teardown
The case metadata drives per-case behavior without needing custom [`Case`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case] subclasses or serialization.

## Conditional Teardown
### Conditional Teardown

The `teardown()` hook receives the full result, so you can vary cleanup logic based on success or failure — for example, keeping test environments up for manual inspection when a case fails. The `result` can be `None` if evaluation is interrupted before the case produces a report result, so handle that branch when your cleanup depends on the case outcome:

## Pydantic Evals
# Pydantic Evals

**Pydantic Evals** is a powerful evaluation framework for systematically testing and evaluating AI systems, from simple LLM calls to complex multi-agent applications.

## What is Pydantic Evals?
## What is Pydantic Evals?

Pydantic Evals helps you:

- **Create test datasets** with type-safe structured inputs and expected outputs
- **Run evaluations** against your AI systems with automatic concurrency
- **Score results** using deterministic checks, LLM judges, or custom evaluators
- **Generate reports** with detailed metrics, assertions, and performance data
- **Track changes** by comparing evaluation runs over time
- **Integrate with Logfire** for visualization and collaborative analysis

## Installation
## Installation

```bash
pip install pydantic-evals
```

For OpenTelemetry tracing and Logfire integration:

```bash
pip install 'pydantic-evals[logfire]'
```

In pydantic-evals, what are the requirements for creating a custom Evaluator so it serializes and deserializes correctly with to_file() and from_file()?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
A custom evaluator in pydantic-evals must be decorated with `@dataclass`, inherit from `Evaluator`, and be passed to both `to_file()` and `from_file()` so it can be handled correctly during serialization and deserialization.

ANSWER A:
# Custom Evaluator Serialization in pydantic-evals

For a custom Evaluator to serialize/deserialize correctly with `to_file()` and `from_file()`, follow these requirements:

## 1. **Inherit from BaseEvaluator**
```python
from pydantic_evals import BaseEvaluator

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    pass
```

## 2. **Use Pydantic-Compatible Types**
All fields must be serializable Pydantic types with proper type hints:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    name: str
    threshold: float
    config: dict
```

## 3. **Avoid Non-Serializable Objects**
- ❌ Don't store: lambdas, file handles, database connections, model instances
- ✅ Store: primitives, strings, dicts, lists, nested Pydantic models

## 4. **Implement Required Methods**
Typically requires implementing the `evaluate()` method:
```python
async def evaluate(self, **kwargs) -> dict:
    # Your evaluation logic
    return {"score": score, "reasoning": reasoning}
```

## 5. **Use Model Config if Needed**
```python
from pydantic import ConfigDict

class MyCustomEvaluator(BaseEvaluator):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=False)
    name: str
```

## Key Principle
**Keep your Evaluator's state purely data-driven** — only store configuration/parameters, not behavior or external dependencies. External dependencies (LLM clients, etc.) should be passed at evaluation time.

QUESTION:
In pydantic-evals, how can I run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare their results in Logfire?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Create a Dataset with your cases, then call dataset.evaluate_sync() separately for each implementation (e.g., report1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(original_task) and report2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(improved_task)). Each run produces its own report, and you can compare them in Logfire by filtering by timestamp or attributes.

ANSWER A:
# Running Multiple Task Implementations Against Same Dataset in pydantic-evals

Here's how to run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare results in Logfire:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Dataset, Task
import logfire

# Initialize Logfire
logfire.configure()

# Create shared dataset once
dataset = Dataset.from_list([
    {"question": "What is 2+2?"},
    {"question": "What is the capital of France?"},
    # ... more examples
])

# Task Implementation 1
class SimpleTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your first implementation
        return {"answer": simple_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Task Implementation 2
class AdvancedTask(Task):
    def run(self, example):
        # Your second implementation
        return {"answer": advanced_llm_call(example["question"])}

# Run both tasks on same dataset with distinct names
results1 = SimpleTask().eval(dataset, name="simple_approach")
results2 = AdvancedTask().eval(dataset, name="advanced_approach")
```

**Key points:**

1. **Create dataset once** - pass the same `Dataset` instance to both tasks
2. **Use distinct `name` parameter** - this identifies each run in Logfire for easy comparison
3. **Logfire automatically captures results** - both runs are logged with metrics, traces, and outputs
4. **Compare in Logfire UI** - view side-by-side results, scores, and performance metrics

In Logfire, you can then:
- View both runs under the same span tree
- Compare success rates, latencies, and outputs
- Filter/search by the `name` parameter to isolate each implementation

Is there a specific comparison metric or evaluation criteria you want to use?

To run two different task implementations against the same Dataset and compare their results in Logfire, follow these steps:

## 1. Configure Logfire Early [3]

```python
import logfire

logfire.configure(
    service_name='rag-pipeline-evals',  # Use a descriptive name
    environment='development',
)
```

## 2. Define Your Dataset and Task Implementations [1]

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='comparison_test',
    cases=[
        Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO'),
    ],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

# Original implementation
def task_v1(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Improved implementation
def task_v2(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper() + '!'
```

## 3. Run Both Implementations and Compare [1][2]

```python
# Run 1: Original implementation
report_v1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v1)

# Run 2: Improved implementation
report_v2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v2)

# Compare results programmatically
avg_v1 = report_v1.averages()
avg_v2 = report_v2.averages()
```

## 4. Compare in Logfire [2]

Both runs will automatically be captured in Logfire. You can then **compare them in the Logfire web UI by filtering by timestamp or attributes** to analyze the performance differences between your implementations.

prompt: In pydantic-ai's pydantic_evals, how can I make sure the experiment metadata recorded by Dataset.evaluate exactly matches the configuration my agent task actually uses?

{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":7579,"duration_api_ms":9714,"ttft_ms":4064,"ttft_stream_ms":876,"time_to_request_ms":6,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Matching Experiment Metadata with Agent Task Configuration

In pydantic-ai's `pydantic_evals`, the key approach is to **explicitly pass your agent configuration to `Dataset.evaluate()` so it's recorded in metadata**:

## Recommended Pattern

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent
from pydantic_evals import Dataset

# Define your agent
agent = Agent(
    model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022',
    system_prompt="Your prompt here",
)

# Define evaluation-specific config
eval_config = {
    'model': agent.model,
    'system_prompt': agent._system_prompt,  # or store separately
    'temperature': 0.7,
    'max_tokens': 2000,
}

# Pass config explicitly to ensure metadata matches
dataset = Dataset.load('your_dataset')
results = dataset.evaluate(
    agent=agent,
    metadata=eval_config,  # Explicitly record what was actually used
)
```

## Key Practices

1. **Store config separately** - Don't rely on implicit agent state:
```python
EVAL_CONFIG = {
    'model': 'claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022',
    'temperature': 0.7,
}

agent = Agent(model=EVAL_CONFIG['model'], ...)
results = dataset.evaluate(agent, metadata=EVAL_CONFIG)
```

2. **Use dataclass/pydantic model for config** - Ensures type safety and easy serialization:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EvalConfig:
    model: str
    temperature: float
    
config = EvalConfig(model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022', temperature=0.7)
agent = Agent(model=config.model, ...)
results = dataset.evaluate(agent, metadata=config.model_dump())
```

3. **Verify metadata post-evaluation**:

parsed_text:
# Matching Experiment Metadata with Agent Task Configuration

In pydantic-ai's `pydantic_evals`, the key approach is to **explicitly pass your agent configuration to `Dataset.evaluate()` so it's recorded in metadata**:

## Recommended Pattern

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent
from pydantic_evals import Dataset

# Define your agent
agent = Agent(
    model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022',
    system_prompt="Your prompt here",
)

# Define evaluation-specific config
eval_config = {
    'model': agent.model,
    'system_prompt': agent._system_prompt,  # or store separately
    'temperature': 0.7,
    'max_tokens': 2000,
}

# Pass config explicitly to ensure metadata matches
dataset = Dataset.load('your_dataset')
results = dataset.evaluate(
    agent=agent,
    metadata=eval_config,  # Explicitly record what was actually used
)
```

## Key Practices

1. **Store config separately** - Don't rely on implicit agent state:
```python
EVAL_CONFIG = {
    'model': 'claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022',
    'temperature': 0.7,
}

agent = Agent(model=EVAL_CONFIG['model'], ...)
results = dataset.evaluate(agent, metadata=EVAL_CONFIG)
```

2. **Use dataclass/pydantic model for config** - Ensures type safety and easy serialization:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EvalConfig:
    model: str
    temperature: float
    
config = EvalConfig(model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022', temperature=0.7)
agent = Agent(model=config.model, ...)
results = dataset.evaluate(agent, metadata=config.model_dump())
```

3. **Verify metadata post-evaluation**:
```python
assert results.metadata['model'] == agent.model
```

This ensures reproducibility and traceability of your evaluations.

# Cost Threshold Evaluator in pydantic-ai with Logfire

Here's how to create a `pydantic_evals` Evaluator that fails when agent run cost exceeds a threshold:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Evaluator

class CostThresholdEvaluator(Evaluator):
    """Fails if estimated cost exceeds threshold"""
    
    def __init__(self, max_cost_usd: float):
        super().__init__(name="cost_threshold")
        self.max_cost_usd = max_cost_usd
    
    async def evaluate(self, run_result) -> bool:
        # Access estimated cost from the agent run result
        # Available when Logfire is integrated
        estimated_cost = run_result.cost
        
        if estimated_cost > self.max_cost_usd:
            print(f"Cost ${estimated_cost:.4f} exceeds threshold ${self.max_cost_usd}")
            return False
        
        return True
```

## Usage Example

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent
import logfire

# Enable Logfire instrumentation
logfire.configure()

# Create evaluator
cost_evaluator = CostThresholdEvaluator(max_cost_usd=0.10)

# Use with agent
agent = Agent(model="gpt-4", name="my_agent")

# In your evaluation pipeline
result = agent.run_sync("prompt")

# Evaluate cost
passed = cost_evaluator.evaluate(result)
```

## Key Points

- **`run_result.cost`** — Available when Logfire is integrated; contains estimated cost in USD
- **Return `False`** — Fails the evaluation
- **Logfire auto-tracks** — Cost metrics are captured automatically when Logfire is configured
- Use **`async def evaluate()`** for async compatibility with pydantic-ai workflows

If cost isn't directly on `run_result`, check Logfire's trace context or use `run_result.metadata` depending on your pydantic-ai version.

[2] (docs/evals/how-to/retry-strategies.md § Evaluator Failures)
## Evaluator Failures
### Evaluator Failures

If an evaluator fails after all retries, it's recorded as an [`EvaluatorFailure`][pydantic_evals.evaluators.EvaluatorFailure]:

```python
from tenacity import stop_after_attempt

from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='evaluator_failures', cases=[Case(inputs='test')], evaluators=[])

report = dataset.evaluate_sync(task, retry_evaluators={'stop': stop_after_attempt(3)})

# Check for evaluator failures
for case in report.cases:
    if case.evaluator_failures:
        for failure in case.evaluator_failures:
            print(f'Evaluator {failure.name} failed: {failure.error_message}')
    #> (No output - no evaluator failures in this case)
```

View evaluator failures in reports:

[3] (docs/evals/online-evaluation.md § Sinks)
## Sinks
```python
from pydantic_evals.online import SinkPayload

class PrintSink:
    """Prints evaluation results to stdout."""

    async def submit(self, payload: SinkPayload) -> None:
        for r in payload.results:
            version = f' ({r.evaluator_version})' if r.evaluator_version else ''
            print(f'  [{payload.target}] {r.name}{version}: {r.value}')
        for f in payload.failures:
            version = f' ({f.evaluator_version})' if f.evaluator_version else ''
            print(f'  [{payload.target}] FAILED {f.name}{version}: {f.error_message}')
```

[4] (docs/evals/online-evaluation.md § Conditional Evaluation)
## Conditional Evaluation
```python
import asyncio
from collections.abc import Sequence
from dataclasses import dataclass

from pydantic_evals.evaluators import (
    EvaluationResult,
    Evaluator,
    EvaluatorContext,
    EvaluatorFailure,
)
from pydantic_evals.online import (
    OnlineEvalConfig,
    wait_for_evaluations,
)

results_log: list[str] = []

async def log_sink(
    results: Sequence[EvaluationResult],
    failures: Sequence[EvaluatorFailure],
    context: EvaluatorContext,
) -> None:
    for r in results:
        results_log.append(f'{r.name}={r.value}')

@dataclass
class ConditionalAnalysis(Evaluator):
    """Runs a cheap check on every call, and an expensive check only on long outputs."""

    def evaluate(self, ctx: EvaluatorContext) -> dict[str, float | bool]:
        output = str(ctx.output)
        results: dict[str, float | bool] = {
            'has_content': len(output) > 0,
        }
        # Only run the expensive analysis on long outputs
        if len(output) > 20:
            # pretend the following line is expensive..
            results['detail_score'] = len(output) / 100.0
        return results

config = OnlineEvalConfig(default_sink=log_sink)

@config.evaluate(ConditionalAnalysis())
async def generate(prompt: str) -> str:
    return f'Response to: {prompt}'

async def main():
    await generate('hi')  # short output — only cheap check runs
    await wait_for_evaluations()
    print(results_log)
    #> ['has_content=True']

    results_log.clear()
    await generate('tell me a long story about dragons')  # long output — both checks run
    await wait_for_evaluations()
    print(sorted(results_log))
    #> ['detail_score=0.47', 'has_content=True']

asyncio.run(main())
```

[5] (docs/evals/core-concepts.md § Evaluation Report)
## Evaluation Report
## Evaluation Report

An [`EvaluationReport`][pydantic_evals.reporting.EvaluationReport] is the result of running an experiment. It contains all the data from executing your task against the dataset's cases and running all evaluators.

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='report_example',
    cases=[Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO')],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

def my_task(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Run an experiment
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task)

# Print to console
report.print()
"""
    Evaluation Summary: my_task
┏━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Case ID  ┃ Assertions ┃ Duration ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ Case 1   │ ✔          │     10ms │
├──────────┼────────────┼──────────┤
│ Averages │ 100.0% ✔   │     10ms │
└──────────┴────────────┴──────────┘
"""

# Access data programmatically
for case in report.cases:
    print(f'{case.name}: {case.scores}')
    #> Case 1: {}
```

parsed_text:
To include evaluator failures in the output when printing an evaluation report, use the `include_evaluator_failures=True` parameter with the `report.print()` method [1]:

```python
report.print(include_evaluator_failures=True)
```

You can also programmatically check for evaluator failures by iterating through the report's cases [2]:

```python
for case in report.cases:
    if case.evaluator_failures:
        for failure in case.evaluator_failures:
            print(f'Evaluator {failure.name} failed: {failure.error_message}')
```

## Pattern 2: Configuration Object (Recommended)
```python
from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass

from pydantic_ai import Agent
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

@dataclass
class TaskConfig:
    """Single source of truth for task configuration.

    Includes all variables you'd like to see in experiment metadata.
    """

    model: str
    temperature: float
    max_tokens: int
    prompt_version: str

# Define configuration once
config = TaskConfig(
    model='openai:gpt-5-mini',
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=500,
    prompt_version='v2.1',
)

# Use config in task
agent = Agent(
    config.model,
    model_settings={'temperature': config.temperature, 'max_tokens': config.max_tokens},
)

async def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    """Task uses the same config that's recorded in metadata."""
    result = await agent.run(inputs)
    return result.output

# Evaluate with metadata derived from the same config
async def main():
    dataset = Dataset(name='config_evaluation', cases=[Case(inputs='What is the capital of France?')])

    report = await dataset.evaluate(
        task,
        metadata=asdict(config),  # Guaranteed to match task behavior
    )

    print(report.experiment_metadata)
    """
    {
        'model': 'openai:gpt-5-mini',
        'temperature': 0.7,
        'max_tokens': 500,
        'prompt_version': 'v2.1',
    }
    """
```

[2] (docs/evals/quick-start.md § Pydantic Evals)
## Pydantic Evals
# Pydantic Evals

**Pydantic Evals** is a powerful evaluation framework for systematically testing and evaluating AI systems, from simple LLM calls to complex multi-agent applications.

## What is Pydantic Evals?
## What is Pydantic Evals?

Pydantic Evals helps you:

- **Create test datasets** with type-safe structured inputs and expected outputs
- **Run evaluations** against your AI systems with automatic concurrency
- **Score results** using deterministic checks, LLM judges, or custom evaluators
- **Generate reports** with detailed metrics, assertions, and performance data
- **Track changes** by comparing evaluation runs over time
- **Integrate with Logfire** for visualization and collaborative analysis

## Installation
## Installation

```bash
pip install pydantic-evals
```

For OpenTelemetry tracing and Logfire integration:

```bash
pip install 'pydantic-evals[logfire]'
```

[3] (docs/agent.md § Systematic Testing with Evals)
## Systematic Testing with Evals
### Systematic Testing with Evals

For systematic evaluation of agent behavior beyond runtime debugging, [Pydantic Evals](evals.md) provides a code-first framework for testing AI systems:

```python {test="skip" lint="skip" format="skip"}
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

dataset = Dataset(
    name='agent_eval',
    cases=[
        Case(name='capital_question', inputs='What is the capital of France?', expected_output='Paris'),
    ]
)
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_agent_function)
```

Evals let you define test cases, run them against your agent, and score the results. When combined with Logfire, evaluation results appear in the web UI for visualization and comparison across runs. See the [Logfire integration guide](evals/how-to/logfire-integration.md) for setup.

[4] (docs/evals.md § Installation)
## Installation
## Installation

To install the Pydantic Evals package, run:

```bash
pip/uv-add pydantic-evals
```

`pydantic-evals` does not depend on `pydantic-ai`, but has an optional dependency on `logfire` if you'd like to
use OpenTelemetry traces in your evals, or send evaluation results to [logfire](https://pydantic.dev/logfire).

```bash
pip/uv-add 'pydantic-evals[logfire]'
```

## Pydantic Evals Data Model
## Pydantic Evals Data Model

Pydantic Evals is built around a simple data model:

## Data Model Diagram
### Data Model Diagram

```
Dataset (1) ──────────── (Many) Case
│                        │
│                        │
└─── (Many) Experiment ──┴─── (Many) Case results
     │
     └─── (1) Task
     │
     └─── (Many) Evaluator
```

[5] (docs/evals/how-to/metrics-attributes.md § Anti-Pattern: Duplicate Configuration)
## Anti-Pattern: Duplicate Configuration
### Anti-Pattern: Duplicate Configuration

**Avoid this common mistake**:

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

# ❌ BAD: Configuration defined in multiple places
agent = Agent('openai:gpt-5-mini', model_settings={'temperature': 0.7})

async def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    result = await agent.run(inputs)
    return result.output

async def main():
    dataset = Dataset(name='anti_pattern', cases=[Case(inputs='test')])

    # ❌ BAD: Metadata manually typed - easy to get out of sync
    await dataset.evaluate(
        task,
        metadata={
            'model': 'openai:gpt-5-mini',  # Duplicated! Could diverge from agent definition
            'temperature': 0.8,  # ⚠️ WRONG! Task actually uses 0.7
        },
    )
```

In this anti-pattern, the metadata claims `temperature: 0.8` but the task uses `0.7`. This leads to:

- Incorrect experiment tracking
- Inability to reproduce results
- Confusion when comparing runs
- Wasted time debugging "why results differ"

# Ensuring Metadata Matches Agent Configuration

Use **Pattern 2: Configuration Object** [1], which creates a single source of truth for your task configuration.

The recommended approach:

1. **Define configuration once** in a `@dataclass`
2. **Use that same config object** for both:
   - Creating your agent
   - Recording metadata via `asdict(config)`

```python
from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass
from pydantic_ai import Agent
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

@dataclass
class TaskConfig:
    model: str
    temperature: float
    max_tokens: int
    prompt_version: str

# Single source of truth
config = TaskConfig(
    model='openai:gpt-5-mini',
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=500,
    prompt_version='v2.1',
)

# Use config to create agent
agent = Agent(
    config.model,
    model_settings={'temperature': config.temperature, 'max_tokens': config.max_tokens},
)

async def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    result = await agent.run(inputs)
    return result.output

# Pass same config as metadata
report = await dataset.evaluate(
    task,
    metadata=asdict(config),  # Guaranteed to match task behavior
)
```

**Why this works:** Since both the agent and metadata derive from the same `config` object, they're **guaranteed to stay in sync**. This avoids the anti-pattern of manually duplicating configuration values, which can diverge [5].

## Without OTel SDK Configured

If no OTel SDK is configured in your process:

- **`gen_ai.evaluation.result` events are emitted but not exported/recorded anywhere** - they become no-ops since there's no active tracer provider to collect them
- No errors occur; the evaluation still runs normally, but telemetry data is lost

## Processing Evaluator Results Directly in Python

Use the **`evaluator` callback parameter** to capture results without OTel dependency:

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent

def my_evaluator(agent_name: str, model_name: str, messages: list, response: str) -> dict:
    # Process evaluation results directly
    return {
        "score": 0.95,
        "feedback": "Good response"
    }

agent = Agent(
    model='openai:gpt-4',
    evaluators=[my_evaluator]  # Callable that processes results
)

result = agent.run_sync("Your prompt")
print(result.evaluation_results)  # Access results directly
```

Or access evaluation metadata from the response object:

```python
response = agent.run_sync(prompt)
if hasattr(response, 'evaluations'):
    for eval_result in response.evaluations:
        print(eval_result.score, eval_result.feedback)
```

You can configure **both** simultaneously:

```python
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider

# Configure OTel SDK
trace_provider = TracerProvider()

# Run agent with evaluators for direct access
# OTel events are exported + you get direct Python results
```

# Pydantic-AI Online Evaluation and OTel Events

## Without OTel SDK Configured

If no OTel SDK is configured in your process:

- **`gen_ai.evaluation.result` events are emitted but not exported/recorded anywhere** - they become no-ops since there's no active tracer provider to collect them
- No errors occur; the evaluation still runs normally, but telemetry data is lost

## Processing Evaluator Results Directly in Python

Use the **`evaluator` callback parameter** to capture results without OTel dependency:

```python
from pydantic_ai import Agent

def my_evaluator(agent_name: str, model_name: str, messages: list, response: str) -> dict:
    # Process evaluation results directly
    return {
        "score": 0.95,
        "feedback": "Good response"
    }

agent = Agent(
    model='openai:gpt-4',
    evaluators=[my_evaluator]  # Callable that processes results
)

result = agent.run_sync("Your prompt")
print(result.evaluation_results)  # Access results directly
```

Or access evaluation metadata from the response object:

```python
response = agent.run_sync(prompt)
if hasattr(response, 'evaluations'):
    for eval_result in response.evaluations:
        print(eval_result.score, eval_result.feedback)
```

## Both OTel + Direct Processing

You can configure **both** simultaneously:

```python
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider

# Configure OTel SDK
trace_provider = TracerProvider()

# Run agent with evaluators for direct access
# OTel events are exported + you get direct Python results
```

**Key point**: Direct Python evaluation callbacks work independently of OTel configuration, so you're not dependent on OTel infrastructure for accessing results.

[1] (docs/evals/core-concepts.md § Evaluation Report)
## Evaluation Report
## Evaluation Report

An [`EvaluationReport`][pydantic_evals.reporting.EvaluationReport] is the result of running an experiment. It contains all the data from executing your task against the dataset's cases and running all evaluators.

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='report_example',
    cases=[Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO')],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

def my_task(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Run an experiment
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(my_task)

# Print to console
report.print()
"""
    Evaluation Summary: my_task
┏━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Case ID  ┃ Assertions ┃ Duration ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ Case 1   │ ✔          │     10ms │
├──────────┼────────────┼──────────┤
│ Averages │ 100.0% ✔   │     10ms │
└──────────┴────────────┴──────────┘
"""

# Access data programmatically
for case in report.cases:
    print(f'{case.name}: {case.scores}')
    #> Case 1: {}
```

[2] (docs/evals/how-to/multi-run.md § Aggregation)
## Aggregation
## Aggregation

With `repeat > 1`, the report's [`averages()`][pydantic_evals.reporting.EvaluationReport.averages] uses a two-level aggregation strategy:

1. **Per-group averages**: Each case's runs are averaged into a group summary
2. **Cross-group averages**: The group summaries are averaged to produce the final result

This ensures each original case contributes equally to the overall averages, regardless of how many runs succeeded or failed.

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='aggregation',
    cases=[
        Case(name='easy', inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO'),
        Case(name='hard', inputs='world', expected_output='WORLD'),
    ],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return inputs.upper()

report = dataset.evaluate_sync(task, repeat=3)

averages = report.averages()
assert averages is not None
print(f'Overall assertion rate: {averages.assertions}')
#> Overall assertion rate: 1.0
```

[3] (docs/evals/how-to/retry-strategies.md § Evaluator Failures)
## Evaluator Failures
```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset

def task(inputs: str) -> str:
    return f'Result: {inputs}'

dataset = Dataset(name='failure_report', cases=[Case(inputs='test')], evaluators=[])
report = dataset.evaluate_sync(task)

report.print(include_evaluator_failures=True)
"""
  Evaluation Summary:
         task
┏━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Case ID  ┃ Duration ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ Case 1   │     10ms │
├──────────┼──────────┤
│ Averages │     10ms │
└──────────┴──────────┘
"""
#>
#> ✅ case_0                       ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 100% (0/0)
```

[4] (docs/evals/core-concepts.md § Multiple Experiments from One Dataset)
## Multiple Experiments from One Dataset
### Multiple Experiments from One Dataset

A key feature of Pydantic Evals is that you can run the same dataset against different task implementations:

```python
from pydantic_evals import Case, Dataset
from pydantic_evals.evaluators import EqualsExpected

dataset = Dataset(
    name='comparison_test',
    cases=[
        Case(inputs='hello', expected_output='HELLO'),
    ],
    evaluators=[EqualsExpected()],
)

# Original implementation
def task_v1(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper()

# Improved implementation (with exclamation)
def task_v2(text: str) -> str:
    return text.upper() + '!'

# Compare results
report_v1 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v1)
report_v2 = dataset.evaluate_sync(task_v2)

avg_v1 = report_v1.averages()
avg_v2 = report_v2.averages()
print(f'V1 pass rate: {avg_v1.assertions if avg_v1 and avg_v1.assertions else 0}')
#> V1 pass rate: 1.0
print(f'V2 pass rate: {avg_v2.assertions if avg_v2 and avg_v2.assertions else 0}')
#> V2 pass rate: 0
```

This allows you to:

[5] (docs/evals.md § Running Experiments)
## Running Experiments
1. Create a [test case][pydantic_evals.dataset.Case] as above
2. Create a [`Dataset`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Dataset] with test cases and [`evaluators`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Dataset.evaluators]
3. Our function to evaluate.
4. Run the evaluation with [`evaluate_sync`][pydantic_evals.dataset.Dataset.evaluate_sync], which runs the function against all test cases in the dataset, and returns an [`EvaluationReport`][pydantic_evals.reporting.EvaluationReport] object.
5. Print the report with [`print`][pydantic_evals.reporting.EvaluationReport.print], which shows the results of the evaluation. We have omitted duration here just to keep the printed output from changing from run to run.

_(This example is complete, it can be run "as is")_

See [Quick Start](evals/quick-start.md) for more examples and [Concurrency & Performance](evals/how-to/concurrency.md) to learn about controlling parallel execution.
